coredata.dc:title: Electron transport in heteroatom-doped graphene quantum dots for TiO2-based dye-sensitized solar cells
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Mahalingam, Savisha
Rabeya, Ramisha
Manap, Abreeza
Lau, Kam Sheng
Chia, Chin Hua
Afandi, Nurfanizan
Omar, Azimah
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) hold promise as co-sensitizers in dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) due to their excellent light-harvesting capabilities. However, their intrinsic limitations in electron transport can hinder overall device performance. This study investigates the impact of heteroatom-doping with nitrogen (N), fluorine (F), and sulfur (S) on the performance of GQDs as co-sensitizers for N719 dye in DSSCs. The heteroatom-doped GQDs (NFS-GQDs) enhance light harvesting compared to pristine GQDs, extending absorption into the UV region. Photoluminescence quenching data confirms efficient electron injection from both GQDs and NFS-GQDs to the TiO2 conduction band, exhibiting superior electron injection efficiency. Among the co-sensitized cells, 20 wt.% doping level achieves the highest power conversion efficiency of 4.33 %. Besides, electron transport and electronic structure were investigated in detail to understand the interaction of the TiO2/NFS-GQDs+N719 interface. The findings suggest that NFS-doping GQDs offer a promising strategy for developing efficient co-sensitizers for DSSCs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electron injection
Energy
NFS-GQD
Recombination

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XPS spectra of GQDs (a) survey scan, (b) C 1s, (c) O 1s.
XPS spectra of NFS-GQDs (a) survey scan, (b) C 1s, (c) O 1s, (d) N 1s, (e) F 1s, (f) S 2p.
UV-vis absorbance of the Sun, N719, GQDs, and NFS-GQDs.
Energy bandgap (Eg) of N719, GQDs and NFS-GQDs.
HR-TEM images of (a) GQDs, (b) NFS(10), (c) NFS(20), and (d) NFS(30), SAED pattern of (e) GQDs, (f) NFS(10), (g) NFS(20), and (h) NFS(30).
(a) Photoluminescence spectra of GQDs and NFS-GQDs at an excitation wavelength of 320 nm. (b) Photoluminescence spectra of NFS(20)-GQDs at various excitation wavelengths.
J-V and power density-output potential difference spectra of reference, GQDs, and NFS-GQDs-based DSSCs.
Quenched photoluminescence spectra of GQDs and NFS-GQDs.
EIS Nyquist spectra of reference, GQDs-based and NFS-GQDs(20 wt.%)-based DSSCs.
EIS Nyquist spectra, and corresponding bode-phase spectra of (a) reference DSSC (b) GQDs-based DSSC (c) NFS(20 wt.%)-GQDs-based DSSC
Electronic structure of (a) GQDs with N719 and TiO2 (b) NFS-GQDs with N719 and TiO2. Atom label: H (white), C (grey), N (blue), O (red), S (yellow), Ti (light grey), Ru (indigo).
Quantum yield of GQDs and NFS-GQDs.
J-V parameters of synthesized and functionalized GQDs.
Electron injection efficiency of GQDS and NFS-GQDs-based photoanodes.
EIS parameters of reference, GQDs, and NFS-GQDs-based DSSCs.

head.ce:title: Electron transport in heteroatom-doped graphene quantum dots for TiO2-based dye-sensitized solar cells

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) hold promise as co-sensitizers in dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) due to their excellent light-harvesting capabilities. However, their intrinsic limitations in electron transport can hinder overall device performance. This study investigates the impact of heteroatom-doping with nitrogen (N), fluorine (F), and sulfur (S) on the performance of GQDs as co-sensitizers for N719 dye in DSSCs. The heteroatom-doped GQDs (NFS-GQDs) enhance light harvesting compared to pristine GQDs, extending absorption into the UV region. Photoluminescence quenching data confirms efficient electron injection from both GQDs and NFS-GQDs to the TiO2 conduction band, exhibiting superior electron injection efficiency. Among the co-sensitized cells, 20 wt.% doping level achieves the highest power conversion efficiency of 4.33 %. Besides, electron transport and electronic structure were investigated in detail to understand the interaction of the TiO2/NFS-GQDs+N719 interface. The findings suggest that NFS-doping GQDs offer a promising strategy for developing efficient co-sensitizers for DSSCs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs), a promising alternative to traditional silicon-based solar cells, have garnered significant attention due to their low-cost fabrication and potential for high efficiency. These devices typically employ a ruthenium-based dye, such as N719, to absorb sunlight and inject electrons into a semiconductor oxide, usually TiO2. However, the efficiency of DSSCs is often limited by the dye's spectral absorption range and the recombination of injected electrons with the redox electrolyte. To expand light harvesting, researchers have explored various methods and technologies, such as additional scattering layers [1], usage of low bandgap materials [2], plasmonic core-shell nanoparticles [3], and many more. While low bandgap materials can potentially enhance light absorption in DSSCs, they often lead to decreased power conversion efficiency (PCE). This is primarily due to the preferential absorption of incident light by the metal oxide semiconductor rather than the dye molecules, which are crucial for efficient electron injection and conversion. Thus, incorporating co-sensitizers can broaden the spectral absorption range and enhance charge transfer dynamics. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have emerged as promising candidates among various co-sensitizers.

GQDs have attracted significant interest as co-sensitizers due to their unique properties, including tunable bandgaps, large surface areas, and potential for upconversion (converting lower-energy photons to higher-energy ones) [4,5]. Rao et al. used GQDs as sensitizers in TiO2-based DSSCs and achieved 2.87 %of PCE [6]. Although the Voc and FF were high, the Jsc remained low [6]. Similarly, Zamiri et al. fabricated a ZnO photoanode with GQDs as the sensitizer and obtained 1.26 % PCE with a low Jsc [7]. It was found that bare GQDs absorb light only in the UV region while the solar irradiates across different wavelengths, including UV light, visible light (sunlight), and near-infrared light. Thus, the small portion of UV light absorbed by the GQDs acting as sensitizers is insufficient, and a need for co-sensitization is vital. Co-sensitization broadens the spectral absorption range of solar radiation. In order to add more functional groups to the bare GQDs, doping GQDs is essential. The influence of different dopant elements on the electronic structure and performance of co-sensitized DSSCs remains an area of active investigation.

Introducing heteroatoms like nitrogen (N), fluorine (F), and sulfur (S) may modify the electronic structure of the GQDs, influencing the bandgap [8,9]. This allows for tailoring the bandgap of the co-sensitizer to achieve a better overlap with the solar spectrum and potentially enhance light absorption complementary to N719. Doping can create additional electronic pathways within the GQDs structure. This can potentially improve the transport of excited electrons generated in the co-sensitizer (GQDs) towards the conduction band (CB) of the TiO2 electrode in the DSSC, leading to more efficient electron injection and overall device performance [10]. Doping might also influence other properties like surface area, surface chemistry, and stability of the GQDs, potentially impacting their interaction with the N719 dye and the overall DSSC performance [10].

Accordingly, Jahantigh et al. used N-GQDs as co-sensitizers with N719 and achieved a high Jsc [11]. The high conductivity of N-GQDs and the overlapping energy levels between N-GQDs and N719 enabled efficient electron transfer from the LUMO of N-GQDs to the CB of TiO2 [11]. Dey et al. used functionalized N-GQDs as the sensitizer and exhibited UV-Vis absorption at the visible region, promoting a high Jsc [12]. They also reported a high stability of the N-GQDs sensitized DSSCs, which did not degrade as quickly as the traditional dyes [12]. However, doping can also introduce complexities. It might create defect states within the GQD structure, acting as recombination centers for electrons and holes, potentially reducing device efficiency [13,14]. Finding the optimal doping level is crucial. Excessive doping can negate the benefits and even worsen the performance. Thus, in this work, we have investigated N, F, and S doping levels in GQDs as co-sensitizers with N719 dye in DSSCs. The novelty of this work offers a strategy to improve light harvesting and overall DSSC performance.

This research directly contributes to Sustainable Development Goals (SDG), SDG 7: Affordable and clean energy by exploring ways to improve the efficiency of DSSCs. By developing more efficient co-sensitizers like NFS-GQDs, this study can contribute to increased use of renewable energy sources like solar power, reduced reliance on fossil fuels and their associated environmental impacts, and development of more affordable solar cell technologies for wider accessibility.

An aqueous solution of NH3 in H2O, NaF, and thiourea is prepared in beaker with a different concentration of 10 wt.%, 20 wt.%, 30 wt.%. 21 mG of cellulose is mixed with 25 mL of distilled water and 1-ethyl-1 methylpyrrolidium bis (trifluoromethyl sulfonyl) imide solvent was prepared separately in a beaker. Then, the aqueous solution (doping precursor solution) and the mixture were combined and sonicated for 30 min to disperse the cellulose and facilitate its breakdown. The sonicated solution was then transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave. The autoclave was heated to 180°C for 24 h for cellulose conversion into NFS-GQDs. The autoclave was allowed to cool down naturally to room temperature. The mixture was then centrifuged to separate the solid product (NFS-GQDs) from the liquid fraction. The solid product was re-dispersed in fresh distilled water using sonication. The centrifugation and re-dispersion steps were repeated several times to remove residual impurities. The re-dispersed NFS-GQDs solution was dialyzed against fresh distilled water using a dialysis membrane for 24 h to remove any remaining small molecule impurities.

The commercial TiO2 paste (Ti-Nanoxide T/SP) from Solaronix S.A. was used to prepare the photoanodes via the doctor blade technique. The paste was deposited on a clean FTO substrate with an active area of 1 cm2 and annealed at 500 °C for 30 min. The annealed TiO2 photoanode was post-treated with TiCl4. Simultaneously, GQDs and NFS-GQDs were co-sensitized with N719 by adding 10 mL with 5 mL of N719 solution (0.5 mM) (Ruthenizer 535-bisTBA -purchased from Solaronix S.A.) and mixing them via a magnetic stirrer for 30 min on a hot plate. Then, the photoanodes were immersed in the mixture of GQDs+N719 and NFS-GQDs+N719 for 24 h at 60°C. Subsequently, the films were cleaned with ethanol to eradicate the surplus dye. A platinum-coated counter electrode is prepared by screen printing a layer of Platisol-T (purchased from Solaronix S.A.) and annealed at 400 °C. Finally, the corresponding DSSC was assembled by sandwiching the sensitized photoanode and the counter electrode, which were laminated using Meltonix 1170-60 (purchased from Solaronix S.A.). The electrolyte (Iodolyte HI-30 -purchased from Solaronix S.A.) was injected through a hole in the counter electrode.

The structural characterization was conducted via a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) (JEOL JEM-2100F, 200 kV). The optical characterizations were compassed using ultraviolet-visible (UV-vis) and PL spectroscopies (Perkin Elmer Lambda 35). The electron impedance spectroscopy (EIS) analysis was conducted using Autolab PGSTAT204. The photocurrent density-output potential difference (J-V) characteristics of the DSSCs have been pursued by a computer-controlled IV Tester System (VS-6820, IVT Solar) at AM1.5 condition and under 100 mW/cm2 illuminated solar light generated from Xenon arc lamp (XES Series Class AAA solar simulator, XES-1600SE-200S). 5 samples were tested per batch for J-V and EIS. The three-dimensional illustrations in the electronic structure of the dye interaction with GQDs and NFS-GQDs were created using the Material Studio tool.

Figs. 1 and 2
show the XPS spectra of GQDs and NFS-GQDs, respectively. More functional groups of COOH are found in NFS-GQDs than the bare GQDs, which are beneficial for sensitizer adsorption on the TiO2 photoanode [10]. Additionally, NFS-GQDs possess extra orbitals of N 1s, F 1s, and 2 sp.

Fig. 1(b) and 1(c) demonstrate the deconvoluted peaks of C1s and O1s, respectively. In Fig. 1(b), the peak around 285 eV indicates the presence of the C=C bond, and the one observed between 288 eV and 290 eV corresponds to the C=O bond [15]. In Fig. 1(c), two notable peaks are observed, where the peak around 529.5 eV and 532 eV denoted C=O bond and that near 533.5 eV refers to C-O bond [16]. Additionally, at approximately 534 eV, the COOH group is identified.

Fig. 2(b) and 2(c) show the separated peaks for C1s and O1s, respectively. The presence of GQDs was confirmed by observing the C=C group peak at around 285 eV, signifying the presence of C-GQDs along with the C=O bond, indicated by the peak between 288 eV and 290 eV. Additionally, the green peak around 286 eV suggests the existence of a C-N bond [17], indicating that the GQDs have been functionalized. The peaks of O1s in Fig. 2(c) showed four prominent peaks around 529 eV, 531 eV, 532 eV, and 533.5 eV, corresponding to the functional groups found in the C-GQDs (C=O and C-O bonds).

Notably, the presence of the three functional groups N, F, and S was detected in Fig. 2(d), (e), and (f) respectively. In Fig. 2(d), a significant peak at 400 eV indicates pyrrolic N, while the peak beyond 402 eV signifies graphitic N, as confirmed by the N-GQDs peak [18]. Additionally, the presence of a C-N bond is determined at approximately 403 eV, indicated by a small green peak [19]. In Fig. 2(e), the peak at 686 eV corresponds to a semi-ionic C-F bond, and the peak at 688 eV refers to a covalent C-F bond, signifying that the GQDs have been functionalized with a solvent containing F atoms. This has been confirmed by the deconvoluted XPS peak of F1s in the functionalized GQDs [20]. In Fig. 2(f), the peak at nearly 168 eV corresponds to a C-S-C bond, and the peak at 170 eV belongs to a C-SO2 bond [21]. These bonding data demonstrate that the GQDs have been functionalized by the NFS-based ionic solvent, with each functional group element having a specific interaction with the GQDs.

The absorption spectra of the Sun, N719, GQDs, and NFS-GQDs (10 wt.%, 20 wt.%, and 30 wt.%) are shown in Fig. 3 . The Sun spectrum data (ASTM G173-03 Reference Spectra Derived from SMARTS v. 2.9.2 Global tilt) was obtained from the National Renewable Energy Laboratory [22].

GQDs and NFS-GQDs have high absorbance in the UV region (less than 380 nm). High absorbance by GQDs and NFS-GQDs in the UV region is typical for these nanomaterials. This strong absorption arises from the electronic transitions within the GQDs structure itself. eVen without doping, bare GQDs inherently absorb strongly in the UV region due to their electronic structure. The overlap of the Sun's spectrum with the UV absorption region of the co-sensitizers indicates that they might harvest some UV light. However, UV light represents a smaller portion of the solar spectrum than visible light. The higher absorbance of N719 across most of the visible region (up to 674 nm) is expected [23]. This characteristic broad absorption allows N719 to efficiently capture a significant portion of the visible light spectrum, which is crucial for efficient light harvesting in DSSCs.

Among the NFS-GQDs, NFS(20) shows the highest overall absorbance. This suggests that the specific doping level (20 wt.%) might have led to a more favorable configuration for light absorption within the GQDs structure. Interestingly, NFS(20) (despite having the highest overall absorbance among the co-sensitizers) shows minimal absorption throughout the visible region. This suggests that while the introduced dopants might enhance UV light absorption, they might not significantly contribute to visible light absorption, which is more critical for DSSCs. NFS(10) shows some visible light absorption up to 470 nm, potentially offering some contribution in this region. However, N719 remains the dominant absorber in the visible range.

Fig. 4 shows the energy bandgap of N719, GQDs, and NFS-GQDs calculated from the Tauc plot method [24]. The bandgap of N719 (3.78 eV) corresponds to strong light absorption in the visible region, which is a desirable property for a DSSC dye. The pristine GQDs (3.85 eV) have a slightly larger bandgap than N719. Due to the larger bandgap, the pristine GQDs (3.85 eV) and NFS-GQDs likely won't absorb any of the same lower energy photons (longer wavelengths) that N719 absorbs. They will primarily absorb higher energy photons in the UV region, corresponding to shorter wavelengths. The bandgap increased slightly with increasing concentration of NFS doping.

Doping with N, F, and S elements introduces foreign atoms into the GQDs structure. These dopant atoms can create new energy levels within the bandgap of the GQDs [25]. The presence of these new dopant-induced energy levels can influence the positions of the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) in the GQDs. Depending on the nature of the dopant states, this can decrease the HOMO-LUMO gap, translating to an increase in the overall bandgap [25]. Higher doping concentrations might increase disorder and defects within the GQDs structure [26]. These defects can act as localized energy states within the bandgap, further contributing to a widening of the bandgap. At higher doping levels, the dopant atoms might start interacting with each other, leading to the formation of clusters or aggregates within the GQDs structure [27]. These clusters can create localized energy states that contribute to the bandgap increase. Therefore, HR-TEM was conducted to observe the GQDs and NFS-GQDs structure.

The HR-TEM images in Fig. 5 show an increase in size from the pristine GQDs to the NFS-GQDs. This is a common observation with doping, as incorporating dopant elements (N, F, S) can increase the overall diameter of the GQDs structure [28]. The ring structure with dots in the SAED pattern of the pristine GQDs indicates a certain degree of crystallinity with some defects. The presence of dots suggests some small, ordered crystalline domains within the GQDs structure. The single crystalline dots and hexagonal shapes observed in the SAED pattern of NFS(10) suggest a higher degree of crystallinity than the pristine GQDs. The hexagonal shape is characteristic of the graphene lattice structure. The diminishing dots and dominance of the ring structure in the SAED patterns of NFS(20) and NFS(30) indicate a possible decrease in crystallinity with increasing doping concentration. The broader ring in NFS(30) might suggest a more disordered structure [29]. The HR-TEM observation of dark spots clumped together in NFS(30) could be due to several factors, such as aggregation [30]. At higher doping levels, the NFS-GQDs might tend to aggregate, forming these clusters of dark spots [30]. The dopant elements themselves might be aggregating within the GQDs structure, appearing as darker regions in the HR-TEM image.

Quantum yield (QY) is calculated using the following equation [31].
(1)
Q Y = Q
Y R
(
I S
I R
)
(
O
D S
O
D S
)
(
η 2
S
η 2
R
)
where S and R represent the sample of GQDs and R is the reference sample of quinine sulfate (0.1 M H2SO4; QY=0.54; λex=350 nm). Meanwhile, I, OD, and η are the photoluminescence, absorbance value, and solvent's refractive index [31].

Table 1 tabulates the resulting QY of GQDs and NFS-GQDs (10 wt.%, 20 wt.%, and 30 wt.%). The NFS-GQDs have higher QY than the bare GQDs due to the presence of surface functional groups that can influence the electronic properties of GQDs, affecting their radiative and non-radiative decay rates [32]. NFS(10) exhibits the highest QY as it has a higher degree of crystallinity compared to the rest, as observed in HR-TEM images (Fig. 5(f)). On the other hand, NFS(30) exhibits the lowest QY due to the aggregation (Fig. 5 (h)). Aggregation can lead to self-quenching, where energy transfer between neighboring GQDs can result in non-radiative decay [33].

Fig. 6 (a) shows the PL spectra of GQDs and NFS-GQDs at an excitation wavelength of 320 nm. Fig. 6(b) shows PL spectra of NFS(20)-GQDs at various excitation wavelengths. The PL intensity is higher for NFS-GQDs compared to the bare GQDs. These dopant states could act as additional radiative recombination centers, leading to a higher PL intensity compared to the pristine GQDs [34]. Compared to the doped variants, the GQDs might have more unpassivated defects, leading to a higher probability of non-radiative recombination and a lower observed PL intensity [35]. Among the NFS-GQDs, the intensity decreases as the NFS content increases. The low PL in NFS(20) may be due to the high aggregation in NFS(30) observed from the HR-TEM image (Fig. 5(d)). If the GQDs aggregate, energy transfer between particles can occur, leading to quenching and also poor QY, as seen in Table 1.

Fig. 7 shows the J-V and power density-output potential difference. The corresponding J-V parameters are presented in Table 2 . The NFS(20)-based DSSC exhibited the highest PCE of 4.33 %. NFS(10) and NFS(20) show the same Jsc of 10.05 mA/cm2. The Voc and FF did not show a significant difference across the DSSCs. The PL quenching effect was studied to analyze the J-V results in detail, mainly the apparent difference in Jsc.

The electron injection efficiency from the sensitizer to the CB of TiO2 was determined by comparing the intensity of the bare sensitizer with that of the TiO2/N719 photoanodes for each sample using the following equation [36].
(2)
P
L
(
T i
O 2
/ N 719
)
− P
L
( x )
P
L
(
T i
O 2
/ N 719
)
× 100 %
where PL(TiO2/N719) is the PL intensity quenching of the reference cell (TiO2/N719), and PL(x) is the quenched sample (TiO2/GQDs+N719; TiO2/NFS(10)+N719; TiO2/NFS(20)+N719; TiO2/NFS(30)+N719). From Fig. 8 , the PL(TiO2/N719) intensity is 50100. The calculated electron injection efficiencies are listed in Table 3 .

The lowest PL intensity suggests highly efficient light harvesting and exciton dissociation within NFS(20). The slightly higher Voc and FF in NFS(20) indicate potentially better charge collection and reduced recombination losses compared to other co-sensitizers, resulting in the highest PCE [37]. The almost similar PL intensity and Jsc to NFS(20) suggest good light harvesting and exciton dissociation in NFS(10). However, the slightly lower Voc and FF might indicate slightly higher recombination losses than NFS(20), leading to a lower PCE in NFS(10). The higher PL intensity suggests a potentially higher rate of radiative recombination within NFS(30). This could be accompanied by lower Voc and FF due to increased recombination losses, explaining its lower PCE compared to NFS(20) and NFS(10). The highest PL intensity aligns with its lower PCE in GQDs. The absence of dopant states and potentially more unpassivated defects [38] could contribute to a lower probability of exciton dissociation and higher recombination, leading to the lowest overall efficiency.

While the trends are clear, there could be some additional factors influencing the performance, such as surface interaction and doping-induced effects. The specific interaction between the co-sensitizer and the TiO2 surface can play a role in electron transfer efficiency. Even with similar Jsc, subtle differences in surface interactions could influence Voc and FF. Thus, electron transport analysis is conducted to investigate how doping influences charge transfer dynamics beyond just affecting exciton recombination.

EIS testing for GQDs and NFS-GQDs-based DSSCs has been conducted to identify the cell's electrocatalytic activity, including charge transfer resistance (Rct), electron lifetime (τn), and frequency (fmax). Fig. 9 illustrates the Nyquist spectra of reference, GQDs, and NFS(20)-GQDs-based DSSCs with the equivalent circuit model. Fig. 10 shows the fitted and Kronig Kramers’ Nyquist spectra. Table 4 lists the corresponding EIS fitting parameters.

Kronig-Kramer's test has been conducted to validate the EIS data, which provides insight into the fitting of the curve and checks if the measured impedance is stable and obtains linearity. The impedance of the imaginary component can be determined from that of the real one, as it defines the mathematical relation between the real and imaginary components. This can suggest how linear, finite, and stable the system is. The validity of the EIS data can be confirmed based on the estimation of the fitting level. It is based on the chi-square values of X2 (Z), X2 (Z’), and X2 (Z”), which are the measure of the fitting quality. X2 lesser than 10−6 ascribes to an excellent fit, whereas 10−5 and 10−6 correspond to a rational fit. A medium fit is indicated by the X2 holding the values between 10−4 and 10−5. All tested DSSCs show medium and rational fits.

All three DSSCs (reference, GQDs, NFS(20)) exhibit relatively low series resistance values (below 6 Ω). This suggests good overall conductivity within the devices, with minimal ohmic losses due to current flow through the electrodes, electrolytes, and internal connections [39]. The Rct represents the resistance to electron transfer from the excited state of the sensitizer molecule to the CB of the TiO2 electrode [40]. The reference cell with the highest Rct exhibits the lowest Jsc, suggesting slower electron transfer [40]. The GQDs-based DSSC shows a moderate Jsc, potentially due to a balance between efficient light absorption by the GQDs and slightly faster electron transfer than the reference. The NFS(20)-based DSSC exhibits the highest Jsc, which correlates with the lowest Rct in EIS, indicating the most efficient electron transfer pathway from the excited co-sensitizer to TiO2.

While the trend in Voc is not as pronounced (Reference ≈ GQDs < NFS(20)), the slightly higher Voc for NFS(20) might be related to minimized recombination losses due to its efficient electron transfer (indicated by low Rct) and potentially better charge collection characteristics [41]. The trend in FF (Reference ≈ GQDs < NFS(20)) suggests slightly improved charge collection and reduced recombination losses in the GQDs and NFS(20)-based DSSCs compared to the reference. Besides, the ring structure with dots in the SAED pattern of the pristine GQDs indicates a certain degree of crystallinity with some defects that could lead to lower Voc and FF. The size difference (GQDs < NFS(20)) in HR-TEM suggests a higher surface area for the NFS(20), potentially leading to more co-sensitizer molecules available for light absorption and electron injection. This might contribute to the higher Jsc observed for the NFS(20)-based DSSC.

The trend in PL quenching (NFS(20) < GQDs < Reference) aligns well with the calculated electron injection efficiency (NFS(20) > GQDs). This suggests that NFS(20) is most efficient at quenching the PL of the GQDs, likely due to efficient electron transfer from the excited state to TiO2. The lower efficiency for GQDs might be due to some radiative recombination losses within the GQDs itself. However, PL quenching doesn't directly translate to overall electron injection efficiency within the device. The combined effect of N719 and the co-sensitizers (GQDs and NFS(20)) should be considered for light harvesting and electron injection. N719 still contributes significantly to light harvesting across a broad range (280 nm - 700 nm). Meanwhile, the roles of co-sensitizers (GQDs and NFS(20)) have become more focused on extending light absorption beyond the range covered by N719. The GQDs primarily contribute in the UV region (up to 400 nm), potentially enhancing light harvesting in that specific range. For NFS(20), while its high UV region absorbance might not significantly affect the overall light harvesting compared to N719, its continued absorbance until 700 nm suggests a potential for extending light capture into the visible region (400 nm - 700 nm).

τn represents the average time an electron remains in the CB of TiO2 before participating in a reaction (either charge collection or recombination) [42]. A longer lifetime might seem desirable, but it needs to be balanced with minimizing unwanted recombination events. All three samples exhibit relatively long electron lifetimes (above 298 ms). Interestingly, GQDs-based DSSC has the lowest τn despite its higher Jsc and PCE than the reference DSSC. A longer electron lifetime doesn't necessarily translate to a more efficient device. While it allows more time for electrons to reach the collecting electrode, it also allows for more opportunities for unwanted recombination events within the device [43]. Once the GQDs were doped with NFS, the τn increased back. Doping could potentially alter the electronic structure of the GQDs, leading to suppressed intrinsic recombination pathways within the doped GQDs material (NFS(20)). The passivation of surface defects on the GQDs surface that might have acted as recombination centers [44]. Doping might influence the energy levels of the NFS in the GQDs. If the doping facilitates faster electron injection from the excited state of NFS(20) to TiO2, it could indirectly contribute to reducing recombination losses within the NFS(20) itself. Faster electron injection would leave less time for unwanted recombination events to occur within the co-sensitizer. However, the electronic structure and recombination pathways of co-doped GQDs need to be investigated to find the potential effects of doping on electron lifetime. Thus, the electronic structure of TiO2/GQDs+N719, and TiO2/NFS-GQDs+N719 was studied.

The electronic structure of TiO2/GQDs+N719, and TiO2/NFS-GQDs+N719 Fig. 11 (a) and (b), respectively. According to the illustration Fig. 11(a), the two bipyridine groups of the N719 dye containing the carboxylic acid (COOH) functional groups will form mono- and bi-dentate bond with the Ti atom presence in the TiO2 surface, which allows the anchoring of the N719 molecules. The GQDs molecules containing aromatic rings in the backbone structure of the quantum dot will interact with the bipyridine group on the N719 to form intermolecular π–π stacking interactions, which can facilitate the electron transfer between these two molecules. In addition, the GQDs molecule containing the OH functional group can act as an active site for electron transfer amid the triiodide particles. In the case of the NFS-GQDs interface with N719 and TiO2 illustrated in Fig. 11(b), the interaction of N719 molecules with TiO2 surface and NFS-GQDs molecules will be the same whereby the bipyridine ring the COOH functional groups which will form a bond with the Ti atom and intermolecular π–π stacking interactions will form between N719 and NFS-GQDs.

When nitrogen is doped with GQDs, GQDs act as electron-withdrawing molecules that accumulate electron that are readily promoted to an excited state during photoabsorption [45,46]. Moreover, upon doping of GQDs with nitrogen, it produces a lane of electrons in the web of TiO2 and reduces electron recombination via the passivation effect at the TiO2/electrolyte interface [47]. When electrons tend to lose energy and return from the CB of TiO2 to the valence band for the purpose of recombination hole, the addition of N doping in GQDs led by its integration with dye incorporated onto TiO2 can trap the electron and inject it faster to the CB of TiO2, preventing it from undergoing recombination. However, provided that the CB of TiO2 is lower than the LUMO of N719 and NFS-doped GQDs. Moreover, the F-doping can favour enhancing dye loading in the TiO2 structure by interacting specifically with TiO2, it produces pores, which provide greater surface area for the dye loading [48]. Furthermore, it helps effectively bind TiO2 with N719 [49], eventually enhancing light harvesting ability. Additionally, the intermolecular π–π stacking interactions between N719 and NFS-GQDs tend to function as a bridge to fasten the injection of electrons into the photoanode, thus reducing Ti3+ trap states, which prevents the electron recombination rate. Besides, the doping of sulfur increases the electronegativity, which acts as a good electrocatalytic activity and high conductivity and for the charge transfer between the redox mediator triiodide molecules [50].

The co-sensitized DSSCs exhibited enhanced light harvesting and improved performance compared to the reference cell containing only N719. UV-Vis data revealed that all co-sensitizers (GQDs, NFS(10), NFS(20), and NFS(30)) contributed to extending light absorption beyond the range covered by N719 alone, potentially enhancing light harvesting in the UV region. The extent of light harvesting in the visible region (400 nm - 700 nm) varied across the co-sensitizers, with NFS(20) exhibiting some continued absorbance throughout this range. The electron injection efficiency followed the trend: NFS(20) > NFS(10) > GQDs, suggesting that co-doping with nitrogen, fluorine, and sulfur plays a role in facilitating electron transfer. Compared to the reference cell (N719 only), all co-sensitized DSSCs exhibited improved Jsc, indicating enhanced light harvesting and electron injection. NFS(20) achieved the highest PCE of 4.33 % among the co-sensitized cells. While electron lifetime data showed some variation, the NFS(20) cell achieved a balance between efficient electron transfer (low Rct) and potentially minimized recombination losses, leading to the highest PCE. The co-doping of nitrogen, fluorine, and sulfur in the GQDs NFS(20) is particularly beneficial. Compared to bare GQDs, NFS(20) exhibited a significant increase in electron lifetime, suggesting potential suppression of recombination pathways within the co-sensitizer itself. For future studies, investigating the long-term stability and durability of the co-sensitized DSSCs under various environmental conditions (light soaking, temperature variations) is crucial for practical applications.

Savisha Mahalingam: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Ramisha Rabeya: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Abreeza Manap: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Kam Sheng Lau: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Chin Hua Chia: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Software, Resources. Nurfanizan Afandi: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Resources. Azimah Omar: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Software, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Ministry of Higher Education, Malaysia, through the Fundamental Research Grant Scheme (FRGS), under the project code of FRGS/1/2023/STG05/UNITEN/02/2.
